1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.

113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).

439	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
440	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
442	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
443	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
444	  % affine patches" (read from
445	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
446	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
447	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
448	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
449	  %   `transitionMap_self d r I : transitionMap d r I I = RingHom.id _`
450	  %   (up to the identification `R^I_I = R^I` from `P^I_I = 1`).
451	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
452	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
453	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
454	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
455	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
456	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
457	\end{lemma}
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
460	  \leanok
461	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
462	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
463	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
464	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
465	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
466	\end{proof}

468	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
469	
470	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
471	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
472	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	[Column submatrix of a product]
476	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
478	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
479	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
480	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
481	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
482	  the right factor:
483	  \[
484	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
485	  \]
486	\end{lemma}
487	\begin{proof}
488	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
489	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
490	\end{proof}
491	
492	\begin{lemma}
493	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
494	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
495	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
496	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
497	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
498	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
499	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
500	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
501	  \[
502	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
503	  \]
504	\end{lemma}
505	\begin{proof}
506	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
507	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
508	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
509	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
510	\end{proof}
511	
512	\begin{lemma}
513	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
514	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
516	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
517	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
518	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
519	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
520	\end{lemma}
521	\begin{proof}
522	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
523	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
524	\end{proof}
525	
526	\begin{lemma}
527	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
528	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
529	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
530	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
531	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
532	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
533	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
534	  \[
535	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
536	  \]
537	\end{lemma}
538	\begin{proof}
539	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
540	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
541	\end{proof}
542	
543	\begin{lemma}
544	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
545	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
546	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
547	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
548	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
549	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
550	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
551	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
552	  defining the chart.)
553	\end{lemma}
554	\begin{proof}
555	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
556	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the order-iso preimage of \(q\);
557	  so the \(I\)-minor is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
558	\end{proof}
559	
560	\begin{lemma}
561	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
562	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
563	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
564	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
565	  The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
566	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
567	  \[
568	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
569	  \]
570	\end{lemma}
571	\begin{proof}
572	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
573	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
574	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
575	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
576	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
577	\end{proof}
578	
579	\begin{lemma}
580	[The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix is the Cramer inverse]
581	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
582	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
583	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
584	  The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
585	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the Cramer inverse:
586	  \[
587	    M_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}.
588	  \]
589	\end{lemma}
590	\begin{proof}
591	  By \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col} the \(I\)-column submatrix of \(M\) is
592	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\), and \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\)
593	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
594	\end{proof}

596	\begin{lemma}
597	[\(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) realises the matrix formula]
598	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
599	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
600	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
601	  Applying the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
602	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) entrywise to the universal matrix \(X^J\) yields
603	  the image matrix:
604	  \[
605	    (\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast\, X^J \;=\; M \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
606	  \]
607	\end{lemma}
608	\begin{proof}
609	  On a free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\) (\(q \notin J\)) this holds by the definition of
610	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as evaluation at the entries of \(M\)
611	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}). On a column \(q \in J\) the entry of \(X^J\) is
612	  an identity-block entry, which \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries to the
613	  corresponding entry of \(M_J = 1_{d\times d}\)
614	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
615	\end{proof}

617	\begin{lemma}
618	[Image of a cross minor under \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)]
619	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
620	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet}
621	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
622	  For a third size-\(d\) subset \(K\), the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) sends the
623	  minor determinant \(P^J_K\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) to the determinant of the
624	  \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\):
625	  \[
626	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr) \;=\; \det\bigl(M_K\bigr).
627	  \]
628	\end{lemma}
629	\begin{proof}
630	  The hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) commutes with determinants and with passing to
631	  a column submatrix, so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
632	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply
633	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}. Proved directly in Lean.
634	\end{proof}

636	\begin{lemma}

638	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}
639	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_eq}

641	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring
642	  homomorphism lying over the structure map \(R^I \to D\), i.e.\
643	  \(\iota \circ (R^I \to R^I_X) = (R^I \to D)\). Writing \(Y\) for the universal
644	  matrix \(X^I\) base-changed to \(D\) and \(Y_X\) for its \(X\)-minor, one has
645	  \[

658	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
659	
660	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
661	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
662	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
663	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
664	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
665	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
666	\[
667	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
668	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
669	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
670	\]
671	
672	\begin{definition}
673	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
674	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
675	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
676	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
677	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
678	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
679	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
680	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
681	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
682	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
683	\end{definition}
684	
685	\begin{definition}
686	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (right)]
687	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_right}
688	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight}
689	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
690	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
691	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} : R[1/y] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
692	  \(x\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
693	  \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(y\) — well-defined since \(y\) is a unit of
694	  \(R[1/(xy)]\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
695	\end{definition}

978	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
979	  % "Therefore the schemes
980	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
981	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
982	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
983	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
984	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
985	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
986	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
987	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
988	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
989	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
990	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
991	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
992	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
993	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
994	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
995	  \[
996	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
997	  \]
998	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
999	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1000	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1001	
1002	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1003	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1004	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1005	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1006	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1007	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1008	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1009	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1010	  follows.
